coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots with 54.4% orange emission for label-free detection of morin and endogenous/exogenous hypochlorite
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Yating
Zhang, Zhuqing
Zhao, Hongxia
Jiao, Yuan
Li, Jun
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots with long-wavelength emission (orange to red), high quantum yield (QY) and good biocompatibility are of great significance for biomedical applications, but achieving this is still a highly challenging task. In this work, multifunctional carbon dots with 54.4% orange emission (O-CDs) were prepared through one-pot solvothermal treatment of nileblueasulphate and citric acid as precursor for label-free recognition of morin and endogenous/exogenous hypochlorite (ClO-) and bioimaging in cellular and zebrafish. Morin can quench the luminescence of O-CDs by static quenching (SQ). The linear range is 5–125 μM and LOD is 0.84 μM. ClO- reduce the photoluminescence intensity of O-CDs via SQ. The linear range is 2.5–90 μM and LOD was 0.46 μM. In addition, The obtained O-CDs have successfully realized the monitoring of morin and endogenous/ exogenous ClO- in living cells and zebrafish owing to its superior biocompatibility, exceptional photostability and lower toxicity. This work opens up a novel opportunity for the development of long-wavelength emission multifunctional nanomaterial with high quantum yield based on CDs for biosensing, biolabeling and biomedical optical imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Orange emission
Multifunctional carbon dots
High quantum yield
Morin
Endogenous/ exogenous hypochlorite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)TEM and HRTEM (inset) image; (B) the diameter distribution histograms; (C) FTIR; (D) Survey of XPS spectra; (E) C1s and (F) N1s spectra of O-CDs.
(A) UV–vis and FL spectra; (B) Excitation-emission matrix of O-CDs.

(A) The photoluminescence titration of O-CDs with addition of morin. (B) Calibration curve of O-CDs toward morin. (C) Linearity relationship of (F0–F)/F0 and morin concentrations. (D) The effect of various amino acids and flavonoids on the PL fluctuation of O-CDs. (E) UV-Vis spectra of morin, O-CDs and O-CDs/morin. (F) Lifetime of O-CDs and O-CDs/morin.

(A) FL titration of O-CDs upon introduction of ClO-. (B) Calibration curve of O-CDs toward ClO-. (C) The fluorescence intensities of O-CDs as a function of ClO- concentration. (D) The effect of various ions on the PL fluctuation of O-CDs. (E) UV−vis spectra of ClO-, O-CDs and O-CDs/ClO-. (F) Lifetime of O-CDs and O-CDs/ClO-.
The LCSM of (a1-c1) O-CDs, (a2-c2) O-CDs/morin in Hela cells. Scale bar: 20 µm.
The LCSM of (a1-c1) O-CDs, (a2-c2) O-CDs/LPS/PMA and (a3-c3) O-CDs/ClO- in Hela cells. Scale bar: 20 µm.
Fluorescence images of zebrafish pretreated with (a1-c1) O-CDs and (a2-c2) O-CDs/morin. Scale bar: 20 µm.
Fluorescence images of zebrafish pretreated with (a1-c1) O-CDs, (a2-c2) O-CDs/LPS/PMA and (a3-c3) O-CDs/ClO-. Scale bar: 20 µm.
Illustration for the synthetic procedure of O-CDs and their application for label-free detection of morin and endogenous/exogenous hypochlorite and bioimaging in cellular and zebrafish.
Comparison of some reported CDs for morin detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CDs = carbon quantum dots; B,N-CDs-B,N dual-doped carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots with 54.4% orange emission for label-free detection of morin and endogenous/exogenous hypochlorite

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National University of Singapore Natl Univ Singapore, Fac Engn, Dept Biomed Engn

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National University of Singapore Natl Univ Singapore, Fac Engn, Dept Biomed Engn, 7 Engn Dr 1, Singapore 117574, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots with long-wavelength emission (orange to red), high quantum yield (QY) and good biocompatibility are of great significance for biomedical applications, but achieving this is still a highly challenging task. In this work, multifunctional carbon dots with 54.4% orange emission (O-CDs) were prepared through one-pot solvothermal treatment of nileblueasulphate and citric acid as precursor for label-free recognition of morin and endogenous/exogenous hypochlorite (ClO-) and bioimaging in cellular and zebrafish. Morin can quench the luminescence of O-CDs by static quenching (SQ). The linear range is 5–125 μM and LOD is 0.84 μM. ClO- reduce the photoluminescence intensity of O-CDs via SQ. The linear range is 2.5–90 μM and LOD was 0.46 μM. In addition, The obtained O-CDs have successfully realized the monitoring of morin and endogenous/ exogenous ClO- in living cells and zebrafish owing to its superior biocompatibility, exceptional photostability and lower toxicity. This work opens up a novel opportunity for the development of long-wavelength emission multifunctional nanomaterial with high quantum yield based on CDs for biosensing, biolabeling and biomedical optical imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: As a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) are utilized in the domains of fluorescent sensing (Meng et al., 2020, 2021a, 2020b, 2020c), drug delivery (Pandey et al., 2013; Tang et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2013), bioimaging (Bourlinos et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2017a, 2017b) and white light-emitting diode (LED)(Chen et al., 2017; Jia et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2017a, 2017b) due to their superb benefits such as abundant low-cost sources (Liu et al., 2018a, 2018b; Yang et al., 2019), lower toxicity (Jiang et al., 2015; Ye et al., 2019), favorable biocompatibility (Ding et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2020) and chemical stability (Bao et al., 2015; Park et al., 2020). However, the emission of most CDs in the short-wavelength region (blue and green), which limits its further biological applications due to the spontaneous blue fluorescence of biological substrates (Shuang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2013, 2019). On the other hand, most of the long-wavelength emission (orange and red) CDs have been reported so far have low quantum yields in water, which is not suitable for bioimaging (Feng et al., 2019; Qin et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). Therefore, it is still a major difficulty to exploit an efficacious strategy for preparing long-wavelength emission CDs with high quantum yield.

Morin ( Scheme 1, 3, 5, 7, 2′, 4′-pentahydroxyflavone), as a kind of natural flavanol, which is widely found in vegetables, fruits and Chinese herbal medicine. Morin's molecular formula it is 3, 5, 7, 2', 4'-pentahydroxyflavone (Sukanya et al., 2018). Morin is widely applied in medicine because of its anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidant, anti-tumor and other advantages. Therefore, the analysis of morin content in biosystems has important value (Liu et al., 2018a, 2018b; Sebastian et al., 2020). At present, the main methods for detection of morin are high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Fallah and Hadjmohammadi, 2020; Fang et al., 2007; Kongkiatpaiboon et al., 2016), electrochemical method (Cheng et al., 2017; Masek et al., 2014; Sasikumar and Ilanchelian, 2017; Sasikumar et al., 2017), fluorescence method and nuclear magnetic method (NMR) (Zielińska et al., 2008). Among them, the fluorescence treatment is considered to be a reliable analysis technique of morin due to its low cost, easy operation, good selectivity and high sensitivity. Toward this end, the morin sensing strategy based on CDs has been developed. Li et al. (2017) initially synthesized CDs with high-efficiency up and -down converted photoluminescence for sensitive detection of morin in a dual-readout assay, which opened a fresh dimension for fluorescence sensing. Subsequently, the strong blue fluorescence CDs were applied as the optical probe for detection of morin in human urine samples (Liu et al., 2019). Recently, our group established a ratiometric sensing strategy of morin based on blue emission B, N-CDs (Yan et al., 2021). However, all of these CDs-based morin analysis methods are limited to the short-wavelength region, which is not conducive to subsequent biological applications. Thence, it is very critical to develop morin detection method based on long-wavelength CDs.

Hypochlorite (ClO-), one of the central reactive oxygen species (ROS), plays a considerable role in biological systems (Huang et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015; Lin et al., 2016). It is a definitive consensus that disordered level of ClO- should be liable for some immunological diseases, such as cancers, arthrophlogosis, cardiovascular disease (Wang et al., 2018, 2019; Zhou et al., 2019). Thus, it is imperative to achieve the quantitative determination of ClO- in biosystem. Recently, many techniques have been developed to detect ClO-, including electrochemistry(KALU, C, 1990), surface-enhanced raman scattering (SERS) (Wang et al., 2016), colorimetric (Sasikumar et al., 2017; Sasikumar and Ilanchelian, 2017) and fluorescent method (Ma et al., 2017). Because of its high sensitivity and selectivity, low detection limit, and easy operation, fluorescent nanoprobes have attracted the attention of researchers. Due to the excellent properties of CDs, some fluorescent nanoprobes based on CDs have been developed. Yin et al. (2013) originally prepared CDs with down-conversion and up-conversion fluorescence for sensitive detection of exogenous ClO- via dual-readout analysis, which provided a new avenue for sensing of CDs. Later, some CDs-based ClO- analysis methods were also established to analyze exogenous ClO- in real samples and cells (Hu et al., 2015; Li et al., 2018; Jiao et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). However, these approaches still confront challenges in performing well on the monitoring of the endogenous ClO- in biosystem. Thus, there is an urgent demand to develop CDs-based fluorescence sensing technique for the detection of endogenous ClO-.

Based on the above considerations, O-CDs were fabricated using nileblueasulphate and citric acid as raw materials by one-pot solvothermal method and were further utilized as fluorescent probes for label-free monitoring of morin and endogenous/exogenous hypochlorite (ClO-). The O-CDs have excitation wavelength independence and high quantum yield (54.4%). Morin and ClO- can decrease the photoluminescence of O-CDs owing to static quenching mechanism (SQ). Moreover, the O-CDs have been successfully applied for monitoring and imaging of morin and endogenous/exogenous ClO- in Hela cells and zebrafish due to their low toxicity and good biocompatibility. As a promising long-wavelength emission multifunctional nanomaterial, the as-prepared O-CDs are expected to be exploited in biosensing, cell labeling and biomedical optical imaging ( Scheme 2).

10 mg nileblueasulphate and 1.5 g citric acid were put in 20 mL ethanol. The mixture solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-equipped stainless autoclave, with heating at 180 ℃ for 6 h. The obtained ethanol solution in autoclave was orderly treated by evaporating ethanol, dissolving in ultrapure water, centrifuging, dialyzing and lyophilizing, and finally the O-CDs powder was obtained.

In the detection of morin, 20 μL of O-CDs (1.0 mg/mL) was mixed with 2.0 mL PBS solution (0.02 mol/L, pH=7.4). Next, 10 μL different contents of morin were added into the O-CDs system and recording the photoluminescence spectrum.
To assess the application of ClO- assay, 10 μL of stock solution of ClO- with different contents was separately added to 2.0 mL of PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4) containing the as-prepared O-CDs.

Human cervical cancer cells Hela cells were hatched in DMEM including 10% FBS. After breeding at 37 ℃ for 0.5 h, the excess medium was eliminated and cells were rinsed three times with PBS (pH=7.4). Cell was treated with fresh culture medium containing O-CDs. After incubation for 3 h, cells were syringed with PBS ( pH 7.4) to remove the remaining O-CDs. Next, cells were incubated for 30 min with morin (0.10 mM). The image was gathered on a confocal laser scanning microscope (LCSM).
Human cervical cancer cells Hela cells were hatched in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium.

(DMEM) involving 10% FBS. The cells were hatched in the aforesaid medium and separated into four groups: the first group was hatched with O-CDs for 0.5 h; the second group was hatched in lipopolysaccharide (LPS, 2.0 μg/mL) and phorbol myristate acetate (PMA, 2.0 μg/mL) for 12 h, followed by breeding with O-CDs for another 0.5 h; the third group was hatched with O-CDs and ClO- (0.10 mM) for 0.5 h. The optical images were gathered after raising the cells by PBS solution for three times.

The O-CDs were synthesized at 180 °C for 6 h by one-step solvothermal treatment, using nileblueasulphate and citric acid dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol (see the Experimental Section for details). In the early stage of the reaction, the hydrothermal temperature, reaction time and contents of nileblueasulphate related to the fluorescence of O-CDs have been discussed in detail. As illustrated in Fig. S1A and Fig. S1B, the fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of O-CDs reach to the optimal values under the reaction temperature of 180 ℃ for 6 h. In addition, the effect of nileblueasulphate content on the fluorescence of O-CDs was demonstrated in Fig. S1C, it can be found that the QY of O-CDs increases gradually with the introduction of nileblueasulphate and attain the maximum with the amount of 0.01 g. The morphology of O-CDs were explored through TEM. Fig. 1A showed the O-CDs were spherical or quasi-spherical with an average diameter of 2.93 nm (Fig. 1B). The interlayer lattice spacing of 0.21 nm indicated by HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1A) was attributed to the (100) face of graphitic carbon.

The multiple functional groups on the surface of O-CDs were confirmed through FTIR assignments (Fig. 1C). O-CDs show a broad peak around 3438 cm−1 corresponding to -NH/-OH stretching vibrations. Peaks at 2987 cm−1, 1738 cm−1 and 1375 cm−1 are assigned to C-H, CO and CN stretching vibration. The bands at 1199 and 1024 cm−1 can be related to C-O stretching vibration. As shown in Fig. 1D, the full XPS spectrum shows three peaks of C1s (283.3 eV), N1s (397.2 eV), and O1s (532.2 eV). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) shows four obvious peaks located at 284.9, 286.0, 287.8 and 288.2 eV, which attributed to CC/C−C, C−N/ C−O, CO and COOH bonds, respectively. The N1s peaks (Fig. 1F) at 398.8, 399.6 and 400.7 eV in Fig. 1F reveal that the N mainly existed in the pyridine N, amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S2) demonstrated double peaks of CO and C-O bands, which situated at 532.0 and 533.4 eV. The zeta potential of O-CDs was 4.82 mV, which further identified the abundant presence of amino groups on the surface of O-CDs (Fig. S3).

The optical behaviors of O-CDs were verified through PL and UV-Vis spectra ( Fig. 2A). Double prominent peaks of UV-Vis spectra were obtained at 255 nm and 523 nm, corresponding to π→π* transition of the CC bond and the n→π* transition of the CO/CN bond. When excited at 514 nm, the N-CDs showed the strongest emission intensity at 615 nm. As similar to many other reported CDs with long-wavelength emissions, the O-CDs exhibited a typical excitation-independent emission behavior (Fig. 2B). Using rhodamine B as the reference, the QY was 54.4% (Fig. S4). There is no obvious fluorescence fluctuation after 60 min of irradiation, which means that O-CD have good photobleaching resistance (Fig. S5). The fluorescence variation of O-CDs to ionic strength was also studied. Fig. S6 shows that the photoluminescence does not fluctuate significantly even at high concentrations (4.0 M NaCl). Furthermore, the influence of pH on the photoluminescence performance of O-CDs are further illustrated in Fig. S7. After treatment with extreme pH (2–12), the fluorescence of O-CDs has no irregular fluctuations. All these results prove the potential application value of O-CDs under physiological conditions.

The surface of O-CDs has a variety of functional groups, so we utilize O-CDs as a effective probe for determination of morin. As illustrated in Fig. 3A, with the addition of morin, the PL intensity of O-CDs gradually reduced. A sigmoidal curve was well fitted using ExpDec equation: y = −0.7745*exp(-x/118.6)+ 0.7909, R2 = 0.9990 (Fig. 3B). The corresponding linearable conjunction was acquired over morin content of 5–125 μM (Fig. 3C, y = 0.0454x+0.0042x, R2 =0.9810) and LOD was 0.84 μM. Besides, we discussed the selectivity of O-CDs for detection of morin. As depicted in Fig. 3D, only morin can quench the photoluminescence of O-CDs. Moreover, in comparison with previous CD-based sensors of morin, the obtained O-CDs exhibit longer wavelength, which gave them worth for subsequent applications in biological systems ( Table 1).

Furthermore, we explored the response mechanism of morin. After adding morin, the UV-Vis absorption of O-CDs changes significantly, and a new absorption peak appears at 348 nm, which is conjectured that the photoluminescence quenching of morin is SQ (Fig. 3E). This inference is further confirmed by the fluorescence lifetime. Comparatively, before and after introduction of morin, the lifetime of O-CDs (Fig. 3F) did not vary obviously (2.78–2.72 ns). At the same time, the quenching constant Kq was calculated as 3.1 * 1012 L/mol/s, much larger than 2.0 * 1010 L/mol/s (Fig. S8). Therefore, photoluminescence quenching is due to static quenching.

Further efforts were made to gain insight into sensing performance of O-CDs. Upon introducing ClO- into the O-CDs system, the photoluminescence intensity of O-CDs regularly decreases ( Fig. 4A). A sigmoidal curve is well fitted using ExpDec model: y = −1.286 *exp(-x/114.5)+ 1.295, R2 = 0.9970 (Fig. 4B). The linear conjunction is received over ClO- content of 2.5–90 μM (Fig. 4C, y = 0.0327x+0.0079x, R2 = 0.9950) and LOD was 0.46 μM. To evaluate the selectivity of O-CDs for ClO-, the photoluminescence response of O-CDs towards different ions (F−, Cl−, CO3 2−, Br−, I−, SO3 2−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, HCO3 −, NO2 −, S2O3 2−, HSO3 −, S2−, H2O2, ONOO-) were inspected. ClO- drops the photoluminescence of O-CDs, while other ions have no influence (Fig. 4D). In addition, in comparison with previous CD-based sensors of ClO-, the obtained O-CDs exhibit longer emission, which gave them value for succeeding appliances in biosystems (Table S1).

To explore the mechanism of ClO- causing fluorescence quenching of O-CDs, the UV–vis spectrum and fluorescence lifetimes were conjectured were consequently investigated. After the addition of ClO-, the UV–vis spectrum of O-CDs changed, and a new peak appeared at 458 nm. Moreover, after the addition of ClO-, the lifetime of O-CDs did not change (2.72–2.72 ns). The quenching constant Kq was calculated as 3.1 * 1012 L/mol/s, much larger than 2.0 * 1010 L/mol/s (Fig. S9). Thence, the quenching mechanism is static quenching.

Good photostability, pH inertness and excellent sensitivity make sure O-CDs an exceptional fluorescent probe in biosystem. We further evaluated the cytotoxicity of O-CDs through MTT (3-(4,5Dimethylthiazol-yl)−2,5Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5diphenyltetrazolium bromide) experiments. The data in Fig. S10 indicated that O-CDs showed no obvious cytotoxicity from 0 to 200 μg/mL, suggesting that the O-CDs with good biocompatibility was suitable for living cells imaging. Thus, O-CDs can be exploited as brilliant optical imaging reagents for analyzing intracellular activities.

For imaging of Hela cells, O-CDs (2 μg/mL) can quickly penetrate the cell membrane, which further proves that O-CDs have excellent biocompatibility. In Fig. 5, with the addition of morin (15 μM), the orange fluorescence of O-CDs is gradually quenched. For monitoring endogenous/exogenous ClO-, the cells were irritated with LPS and PMA for 12 h to generate endogenous ClO- and NaClO (10 μM) was added to culture medium to supply exogenous ClO-, then hatched with O-CDs for 0.5 h. As displayed in Fig. 6, both endogenous/exogenous ClO- can weaken the orange fluorescence of O-CDs. The result is consistent with the results of extracellular experiments, indicating the latent use of O-CDs in the field of intracellular morin and ClO- related diseases.

We further explored the potential applications of O-CDs in vivo. Zebrafish was chosen for in-vivo imaging because it had proven to be an extremely valuable vertebrate model in numerous biological studies. As shown in Fig. 7, the zebrafish have bright orange fluorescence in their bodies. After adding morin (15 μM), their fluorescence is significantly reduced. Fig. 8 shows that after the ZF are incubated with O-CDs (2 μg/mL)/LPS/PMA and O-CDs (2 μg/mL)/NaClO (10 μM), the fluorescence decreases significantly, which proves that O-CDs have excellent performance in the monitoring of endogenous/exogenous ClO- in vivo. All of results indicate that O-CDs have successfully achieved the detection of morin and endogenous/exogenous ClO- in living cells and zebrafish.

In summary, multifunctional carbon dots with 54.4% orange emission (O-CDs) were prepared by one-pot solvothermal treatment of nileblueasulphate and citric acid as precursor for label-free monitoring of morin and endogenous/exogenous hypochlorite (ClO-) and bioimaging in cellular and zebrafish. Morin and ClO- can drop the photoluminescence of O-CDs owing to static quenching. In addition, The as-proposed O-CDs have successfully achieved the monitoring of morin and endogenous/ exogenous ClO- in living cells and zebrafish on the basis of its superior biocompatibility, exceptional photostability and lower toxicity. This work opens up a novel opportunity for the development of long-wavelength emission multifunctional nanomaterial with high quantum yield based on CDs for biosensing, biolabeling and biomedical optical imaging.

Yating Meng: Analyzed the data, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zhuqing Zhang: Investigation, Discussed the results. Hongxia Zhao: Formal analysis, Discussed the results. Yuan Jiao: Project administration, Supervision. Jun Li: Supervision. Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision. Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 and 91843301).
